coredata.dc:title: Novel pillar[5]arene-based supramolecular organic framework gel for ultrasensitive response Fe3+ and F− in water
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, You-Ming
He, Jun-Xia
Zhu, Wei
Li, Yong-Fu
Fang, Hu
Yao, Hong
Wei, Tai-Bao
Lin, Qi
coredata.dc:description: A novel supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) has been constructed by a novel bilateral 8-hydroxyquinoline modified pillar[5]arene host (P5Q) and a bilateral bromohexyl functionalized pillar[5]arene guest (P5C6). Interestingly, the gel PQPA shows special selective and ultrasensitive sensing property for Fe3+. The limit of lowest detection (LOD) of gel PQPA for Fe3+ is 0.102 nM. Meanwhile, by adding Fe3+ into the gel PQPA, a no fluorescence Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) could be constructed. Due to the competitive coordination of F−, fluorescent “turn-on” ultrasensitive response of the Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) for F− is achieved. The limit of lowest detection of the Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) for F− is 9.79 nM. In addition, the xerogel of the supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) is capable for removing Fe3+ in a lower concentration aqueous solution at an adsorption rate of 99.9%. More importantly, the supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) could be used to detect Fe3+ in the Yellow River Water samples. The supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) and Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) could act as ultrasensitive reversible fluorescent chemosensor, test kits and fluorescent display materials for Fe3+ and F−.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasensitive detection
Supramolecular organic framework gel
Fluoride anion
Iron (III) ion
Effective removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasensitive response of PQPA for Fe3+
Ultrasensitive response of PQPA-Fe for F−
Effective removal of Fe3+ from water by PQPA
Detection of Fe3+ with PQPA in the Yellow River Water
Application of PQPA to reversible fluorescent chemosensor
Application of PQPA and PQPA-Fe as ions test kits and fluorescent display materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cartoon illustration of assembly process for supramolecular organic framework gel PQPA and its ultrasensitive response mechanism.
Fluorescence emission spectra of PQPA (gel and sol state) in cyclohexanol solution (15% w/v).

Bar graphs of fluorescence intensity (λ em = 470 nm) (a) adding of various cations into PQPA, and (b) adding of various anions into PQPA-Fe; photographs of (c) adding of various cations into PQPA, and (d) adding of various anions into PQPA-Fe under 365 nm UV light (a and c from left to right is free PQPA, Fe3+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cr3+ and Pb2+, b and d from left to right is free PQPA-Fe, F−, AcO−, ClO4 −, CN−, Cl−, SCN−, HSO4 −, H2PO4 −, Br− and I−).
Fluorescence emission spectra adding of various concentrations of Fe3+ (0–0.18 equiv.) into PQPA.
Fluorescence emission spectra adding of various concentrations of F− (0–0.86 equiv.) into metallogel PQPA-Fe.
Partial 1H NMR spectra of P5Q in DMSO‑d 6 with different equivalent P5C6 (a) free P5Q; (b) free P5C6; (c) 1.0 equiv.; (d) 2.0 equiv.; (e) 3.0 equiv.
FT-IR spectra of powder P5Q and xerogel PQPA.
XRD patterns of powder P5Q, xerogel PQPA, xerogel PQPA-Fe, and xerogel PQPA-Fe + F−.
Fluorescence emission spectra (λ ex = 290 nm) adding various concentrations of Fe3+/river water into PQPA (in cyclohexanol, 15%).
The changes of fluorescence intensity (λ em = 470 nm) alternately adding Fe3+/F− into PQPA.

Photos of (a) the PQPA gel thin film, (b) Fe3+ was added onto the PQPA gel thin film, (c) PQPA-Fe metallogel thin film, (d) F− was added onto the PQPA-Fe metallogel thin film. The photographs were taken at room temperature under nature a 365 nm ultraviolet light (UV).
Comparison of LOD with some reported of sensing systems for Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Novel pillar[5]arene-based supramolecular organic framework gel for ultrasensitive response Fe3+ and F− in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China, Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, Gansu 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environment-Related Polymer Materials, Ministry of Education of China
Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou City University, Lanzhou, Gansu 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) has been constructed by a novel bilateral 8-hydroxyquinoline modified pillar[5]arene host (P5Q) and a bilateral bromohexyl functionalized pillar[5]arene guest (P5C6). Interestingly, the gel PQPA shows special selective and ultrasensitive sensing property for Fe3+. The limit of lowest detection (LOD) of gel PQPA for Fe3+ is 0.102 nM. Meanwhile, by adding Fe3+ into the gel PQPA, a no fluorescence Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) could be constructed. Due to the competitive coordination of F−, fluorescent “turn-on” ultrasensitive response of the Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) for F− is achieved. The limit of lowest detection of the Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) for F− is 9.79 nM. In addition, the xerogel of the supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) is capable for removing Fe3+ in a lower concentration aqueous solution at an adsorption rate of 99.9%. More importantly, the supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) could be used to detect Fe3+ in the Yellow River Water samples. The supramolecular organic framework gel (PQPA) and Fe3+ coordinated metallogel (PQPA-Fe) could act as ultrasensitive reversible fluorescent chemosensor, test kits and fluorescent display materials for Fe3+ and F−.

Novel supramolecular organic framework gel PQPA was constructed by functionalized pillar[5]arenes. Interestingly, the SOF gel PQPA could continuously and ultrasensitively detect Fe3+ and F− in water.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, ultrasensitive response materials attracted more and more attentions due to its high sensitivity for target guest [1–3]. Moreover, it's very important to detect very low concentration target analyte in environment or biosystem. It is well known that some ions are closely related to ecological environment and life system. So, detection and separation of toxic and/or important ions and metallic species are very valuable [4–6]. Fe3+, one of the most important transition metal ions, is the most abundant and versatile metal ions in living organisms [7,8]. Howbeit, Fe3+ deficiency or excess can provoke serious biological disorders and diseases, such as anemia, cancers and dysfunction of organs [9,10]. In addition, F−, the essential micronutrients, are widely present in the environment and in the human body [11], but undue fluoride will not only pollute the environment but also cause human diseases (e.g. kidney problems, acute gastric disorders, dental and skeletal fluorosis) [12,13]. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop some new and simple methods for ultrasensitive detection Fe3+ and F−.

Over the past twenty years, framework materials (MOFs, COFs) [14,15] has attracted the interest of researchers due to their novel structure and wide application in heterogeneous catalysis [16], drug delivery [17,18] and gas storage and separation [19]. Supramolecular organic frameworks (SOFs) are a new and striking research topic in framework materials. They are constructed by weak interaction between molecules (van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, π⋯π stacking etc.) [20,21]. So far, a number of different organic molecules are used to construct SOFs. For instance, Zhang et al. [22] reported a cucurbit[8]uril-based 2D SOF, and applied to the high selectivity and sensitive recognition of picric acid. Tan et al. [23] reported a pillar[n]arene-based SOF, and study its ability to store and selectively adsorb hydrocarbon compounds. Therefore, the rapid development of SOFs provided a new platform for obtaining ultrasensitive materials.

Macrocyclic hosts (crown ether [24,25], cyclodextrins [26,27], calixarenes [28,29] and cucurbiturils [30]) are important candidates for the construction of supermolecular systems thanks to their unique structure and excellent performance. Pillar[n]arenes, a new type of macrocyclic host molecules, which were discovered by Ogoshi and co-workers in 2008 [31]. In recent years, various groups connected to pillar[n]arenes and obtained a variety of functionalized pillar[n]arenes. Functionalized pillar[n]arenes can exhibit unique and extensive host-guest recognition properties [32,33], as a result, many of them were used to construction of a variety of laudable supermolecular systems [34,35] such as chemosensors [36–38], transmembrane channels [39,40], catalysis [41], supramolecular polymers [42] and supramolecular organic frameworks (SOFs) [43]. This is also a major breakthrough of host-guest chemistry.

Based on these backgrounds, we fabricated a novel supramolecular organic framework gel PQPA for ultrasensitive detection of Fe3+ and F− in water (Fig. 1 ). The SOF gel PQPA was constructed from a novel bilateral 8-hydroxyquinoline modified pillar[5]arene host (P5Q) and a bilateral bromohexyl functionalized pillar[5]arene guest (P5C6) by non-covalent interactions (CH⋯O, CH⋯π and π⋯π etc.). Interestingly, with the assembly of P5Q and P5C6, the obtained the gel PQPA showed strong pale blue fluorescence emission. Excitingly, gel PQPA exhibited ultrasensitive response and removal of Fe3+. Meanwhile, by adding Fe3+ into the gel PQPA, a no fluorescence metallogel PQPA-Fe could be constructed. The metallogel PQPA-Fe could carry out ultrasensitive detection of F−. Finally, the gel PQPA was used to detect the Fe3+ concentration in the Yellow River Water. Moreover, the SOF gel PQPA and metallogel PQPA-Fe could act as ultrasensitive reversible fluorescent chemosensor, test kits and fluorescent display materials for Fe3+ and F−.

Besides the Yellow River Water, two distilled water had been used in the experimental process. Other reagents and raw materials all analysis level, does not need further to purify then use. In ions recognition experiments, metal ions had been used as perchlorate salts and anions had been used as tetrabutylammonium salts.

The gel PQPA was prepared by dissolving P5Q (0.030 g) and P5C6 (0.028 g) in cyclohexanol solution (0.2 mL) and the temperature is not lower than 46 °C followed by cooling to room temperature. Then, we investigated the metal ions response properties of the gel PQPA via the addition and diffusion of 1.0 equiv. of various metal ions, including Fe3+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cr3+ and Pb2+ (water solution, c = 0.1 M) into the gel PQPA (w/v, 15%), respectively. Then, any changes in the fluorescence spectra of the gel PQPA upon the addition of different metal ions were recorded.

Varian Mercury 400 or Varian Inova 600 instruments were used for obtaining 1H NMR or 13C NMR spectra. MS spectra were recorded on a Bruker Esquire 6000 MS instrument. Single crystal X-ray diffraction data was collected on a transmission mode with a Rigaku RINT2000 diffractometer equipped, using graphite monochromated CuKa (λ = 1.54073 Å) radiation. X-4 digital melting-point apparatus were used for melting points. IR spectra were performed on a Digilab FTS-3000 FTIR spectrophotometer. By field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM-6701F, accelerating voltage of 5 kV) had been used to characterize morphologies and sizes of xerogels. Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrophotometer was used for recording fluorescence spectra.

The compounds P5C4 (bilateral bromobutyl functionalized pillar[5]arene) and P5C6 were synthesized according to published procedures [44,45]. As shown in Scheme 1 , P5C4 (0.99 g, 1 mmol), KI (0.08 g, 0.5 mmol), K2CO3 (1.38 g, 10 mmol) and 8-hydroxyquinoline (0.36 g, 2.5 mmol) was dissolved in acetonitrile (60.0 mL). The reaction mixture was refluxed for 60 h under nitrogen atmosphere. After the solvent was evaporated and subjected to silica gel chromatography (petroleum ether/ethyl acetate = 5:1, v/v) to give the target compound P5Q as pale-yellow solid (0.57 g, yield 50.8%), mp: 97–100 °C. 1H NMR (CDCl3, 600 MHz), δ/ppm: 8.95 (dd, J 1 = 4.1 Hz, J 2 = 1.6 Hz, 2H), 8.12 (dd, J 1 = 8.3 Hz, J 2 = 1.5 Hz, 2H), 7.45–7.38 (m, 6H), 7.08 (d, J = 7.6 Hz, 2H), 6.80–6.74 (m, 10H), 4.36–4.32 (m, 4H), 3.93 (m, 4H), 3.85–3.74 (m, 10H), 3.65–3.58 (m, 24H), 2.28–2.24 (m, 4H), 2.10–2.05 (m, 4H). 13C NMR (CDCl3, 151 MHz), δ/ppm: 154.73, 150.66, 149.29, 140.41, 135.87, 128.19, 126.64, 119.59, 113.66, 108.71, 68.51, 67.81, 55.67, 55.66, 55.61, 29.58, 29.46, 26.56, 26.00. MS m/z: calcd for C69H72N2O12 [P5Q+H]+: 1121.51; found: 1121.52 (Figs. S1–S3).

The gelation abilities of the host P5Q and guest P5C6 were tested in various solvents via “stable to inversion of a test tube” method (Table S1). Entertainingly, an AIEE (aggregation-induced emission enhancement) effect was observed for the gel PQPA [46]. As shown in Fig. 2 , after switching of the gel PQPA to its sol state by heating, the PQPA sol has weak fluorescence emission under UV light (365 nm). However, when the sol state of PQPA is converted to its gel state by cooling, the PQPA gel shows strong pale blue fluorescent emission under UV light (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasensitive response of PQPA for Fe3+
Ultrasensitive response of PQPA-Fe for F−

ce:section.ce:para: Due to anti-interference is an important aspect for supramolecular chemical sensors, therefore, the anti-interference property of the gel PQPA for response Fe3+ were examined under the condition of coexisting other metal ions. First, equivalent Fe3+ and different metal ions (Ca2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cr3+ and Pb2+) were added to the gel PQPA (15%, 200 μL), respectively. Then, their fluorescence intensity values were recorded independently. As shown in Fig. S10, coexisting of above competition metal ions didn't show distinct interference on the Fe3+ sensing process. The same method was used to test the interference of presence of other anions (AcO−, ClO4 −, CN−, Cl−, SCN−, HSO4 −, H2PO4 −, Br− and I−) for the metallogel PQPA-Fe response F−. As shown in Fig. S11, coexisting of other anions didn't show distinct interference on the F− sensing process. These results indicated that the gel PQPA for Fe3+ and metallogel PQPA-Fe for F− have specific selectivity.

The assembly mechanism of P5Q and P5C6 was studied through various characterization techniques. First, the host-guest interaction between P5Q and P5C6 was analyzed by 1H NMR titration experiment. As shown in Fig. 6 , when separately adding 1 equivalent, 2 equivalent, and 3 equivalent P5C6 to a certain concentration (8.9 mM) of P5Q solution, the resonances of H1, H2 and H3 protons on P5C6 showed upfield shifts, and the signals protons Hg, Hh and Hi protons on P5Q showed downfield shifts. The above results indicated that part of alkyl chain of guest P5C6 enters cavity of host P5Q, and they are bound by CH⋯π and CH⋯O interactions. Meanwhile, the signals of Ha–f protons on the 8-hydroxy quinoline group of P5Q also exhibited slight upfield shifts. This indicated that π⋯π stacking interactions were involved in the assembly process [45,58].

In addition, concentration-dependent 1H NMR and 2DNOESY experiments between P5Q and P5C6 were also carried out. In Fig. S12, overlapping peaks A, B and C represent the correlation between protons Ha–i on P5Q and protons H1~3 on P5C6. In Fig. S13, the concentration of both increased from 4.6 mM to 13.6 mM, all signals of protons become wider. The above experiments results indicated that the SOF gel PQPA was constructed by CH⋯π, CH⋯O and “exo-wall π⋯π” interactions between P5Q and P5C6.

By IR, XRD and SEM analysis method studied the ultrasensitive response mechanism of the gel PQPA for Fe3+ and the metallogel PQPA-Fe for F−. The IR spectra of xerogel PQPA, xerogel PQPA-Fe and xerogel PQPA-Fe + F− are shown in Fig. S14. Compared to xerogel PQPA, the vibration absorption peaks of xerogel PQPA-Fe of CN moved from 1693 cm−1 to 1665 cm−1 and became a moderate peak. As expected, the CN vibration absorption peak of xerogel PQPA-Fe + F− recovered to 1693 cm−1. Moreover, the XRD pattern of xerogel PQPA-Fe and xerogel PQPA-Fe + F− are shown in Fig. 8. Peaks of xerogel PQPA showed at 2θ = 23.64°, 23.93°, 24.61° and 25.50° indicating the existence of π⋯π stacking interactions. These peaks of xerogel PQPA-Fe reduced. In xerogel PQPA-Fe + F−, these strong peaks appeared again. As shown in Fig. S15, the SEM image demonstrates that xerogel PQPA showed lamellar stacking structure. This structure was destroyed in the PQPA-Fe xerogel, while in xerogel PQPA-Fe + F−, the image again presented lamellar stacking.

The experimental results of IR, XRD and SEM all showed that the gel PQPA can provide ultrasensitive response for Fe3+, through the competition between “exo-wall π⋯π” interactions and cation…π interactions. And the metallogel PQPA-Fe can provide ultrasensitive response for F− by competitive coordination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effective removal of Fe3+ from water by PQPA
ce:section.ce:para: We studied the removal performance of xerogel PQPA for Fe3+ in water. First, aqueous solution of Fe3+ (c = 1.0 × 10−5 M, v = 5.0 mL) was prepared. Then, the xerogel PQPA (0.10 mg) was added to the above dilute aqueous solution of Fe3+. Besides, the supernatant was taken after shaking and centrifugation. The ICP (Inductively Coupled Plasma) analysis showed that the residual concentration of Fe3+ in water was about 1.426 × 10−3 mg/L (Table S2). Experimental analysis results indicated that 99.9% of Fe3+ could be efficiently removed even in extremely dilute solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ with PQPA in the Yellow River Water
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the river water (Yellow River Water) was used to test the applicability of the gel PQPA. Added different concentrations of Fe3+ aqueous solution and river water into gel PQPA, severally. As shown in Fig. 9 , after addition of river water the fluorescence emission peak of gel PQPA is closest to the fluorescence emission peak after addition of Fe3+ at a concentration of 1.0 × 10−6 M. ICP analysis verified that the concentration of Fe3+ in river water was about 1.13 × 10−6 M (Table S3). Apparently, gel PQPA can successfully detected concentration of Fe3+ in river water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PQPA to reversible fluorescent chemosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The gel PQPA could selectively sense Fe3+, and the metallogel PQPA-Fe could selectively respond to F−. This property made gel PQPA and metallogel PQPA-Fe act as Fe3+ and F− controlled reversible fluorescent chemosensor. As shown in Fig. 10 , alternately added Fe3+ and F− into gel PQPA, the “on-off-on” process could be reversibly performed at least for three cycles. After recycle experiment, the concentration of the gel PQPA decreases with the continuous addition of ions, so the fluorescence intensity decreases slightly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PQPA and PQPA-Fe as ions test kits and fluorescent display materials
ce:section.ce:para: The gel PQPA and metallogel PQPA-Fe were placed onto clean glass plate and the corresponding thin films were prepared (Fig. 11a and c). They can more easily and quickly detect Fe3+ and F−. As shown in Fig. 11b, when Fe3+ aqueous solution was used to write on the PQPA gel thin film, the characters can be clearly observed under UV light of 365 nm. Similarly, when F− aqueous solution was used to write on the no fluorescence PQPA-Fe metallogel thin film, the words with pale blue fluorescence can be seen under UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 11b). So, the gel PQPA and metallogel PQPA-Fe thin film could act as test kits for these ions as well as fluorescent display materials.

In brief, a unique SOF gel PQPA has been perfectly constructed using the host-guest interaction between P5Q and P5C6. Interestingly, the gel PQPA showed ultrasensitive response and removal of Fe3+ in water. In addition, by adding Fe3+ into the gel PQPA, a no fluorescence metallogel PQPA-Fe could be constructed. The metallogel PQPA-Fe exhibited fluorescent “turn-on” ultrasensitive detection of F− in water. More significantly, the xerogel PQPA could effectively remove Fe3+ from water. And gel PQPA could be used to detect concentration of Fe3+ in the Yellow River Water. Finally, the SOF gel PQPA and metallogel PQPA-Fe be used as ultrasensitive reversible fluorescent chemosensor, test kits as well as fluorescent display materials for Fe3+ and F−.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21661028; 21662031; 21574104), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University of Ministry of Education of China (IRT 15R56) for their financial support for this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.02.094.
